food_subgroup: Fried potato-based snacks with cheese sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for french fries (FOODON_00002670) but no dedicated entry for cheese fries as a composite dish; closest_parent match used with cheese sauce component noted separately.
ontology.foodex2_description: Potato chips / crisps and similar fried potato products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried potato snack products; no dedicated composite code exists for cheese fries. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for cheese fries as a composite food item.

serving.description: 1 standard serving of fries with cheese
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of cheese fries is approximately 250 g, equivalent to a medium-to-large fast-food portion of deep-fried potato strips topped with nacho or processed cheese sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese fries — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database (core macros); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, french fries with cheese sauce, FDC ID 173944 (secondary micronutrients cross-reference)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 255 kcal, protein 4.52 g, fat 14.56 g, carbohydrate 26.46 g, sugar 1.37 g, sodium 397 mg, dietary fibre 2.3 g) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium 60 mg, iron 0.6 mg, cholesterol 16 mg, saturated fat 6.0 g, trans fat 0.2 g, vitamin C 4.0 mg) were originally cited from a delivery-platform source in the input record — a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy — and have been replaced with values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173944 (french fries with cheese sauce) as the closest approved-source equivalent. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where no approved-source data was available for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; deep-fried french fries GI entry (GI 75, glucose reference scale)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 (glucose reference scale) is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care international GI tables for deep-fried french fries. Cheese fries are not independently listed in any approved GI source. The GI is estimated as equivalent to plain deep-fried french fries because the cheese sauce contributes negligible additional carbohydrate and does not meaningfully alter the glycaemic response of the potato base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (75) × carbohydrate per serving (26.46 g/100 g × 250 g = 66.15 g) / 100 = 49.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 397 mg/100 g combined with saturated fat 6 g/100 g from deep-frying and processed cheese sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cheese fries; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 993 mg sodium (41% of the 2400 mg daily limit) and 15 g saturated fat, both of which elevate blood pressure and arterial stiffness — if consumed, request cheese sauce on the side and limit portion to no more than 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) with 26.5 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 2.3 g dietary fibre providing minimal glucose buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid cheese fries; the high GI of deep-fried potato (GI ~75) combined with a 250 g serving delivering approximately 66 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~50 will cause a significant post-meal blood glucose excursion that is difficult to manage with standard insulin or oral hypoglycaemic regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6 g/100 g, trans fat 0.2 g/100 g, cholesterol 16 mg/100 g, and ultra-processed deep-fried preparation generating dietary advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid cheese fries; a 250 g serving provides 15 g saturated fat (75% of the 20 g daily limit), 0.5 g trans fat (exceeding the WHO recommended maximum of 0 g), and significant AGE load from deep-frying, all of which promote LDL oxidation, endothelial dysfunction, and atherosclerotic progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; trans fat present; potatoes are a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid cheese fries entirely; deep-frying generates high levels of AGEs that directly amplify synovial inflammation, trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory at any dose, and potatoes as a nightshade vegetable may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese fries are a popular Western fast-food and casual-dining snack consisting of deep-fried potato strips (french fries) topped with melted processed cheese sauce or nacho-style cheese sauce. The dish originates from American fast-food culture and is widely served in Singapore at fast-food chains, casual restaurants, and food delivery platforms. The fries are typically cut from starchy potatoes, deep-fried in vegetable oil at high temperature until golden and crispy on the outside with a soft interior, then generously coated or drizzled with a warm, viscous processed cheese sauce made from cheddar or American-style cheese, emulsifiers, and sodium-containing stabilisers. The flavour profile is savoury, salty, and rich, with the umami of the cheese sauce complementing the starchy, slightly oily potato. Nutritionally, cheese fries are energy-dense at 255 kcal/100 g, with a high fat content of 14.56 g/100 g (of which 6 g is saturated fat and 0.2 g is trans fat), moderate carbohydrate at 26.46 g/100 g, and moderate protein at 4.52 g/100 g. Sodium is elevated at 397 mg/100 g, making a standard 250 g serving a significant sodium load (~993 mg). The glycemic index is high (~75, glucose scale) due to the deep-fried potato base, and the glycemic load per standard serving is approximately 50, placing this dish in the high-impact category for blood glucose management. Dietary fibre is low at 2.3 g/100 g, providing minimal glucose buffering. This dish is classified as ultra-processed and is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is vegetarian but not vegan, halal status is unverified, and it contains dairy allergens and potentially wheat from the cheese sauce.
common_user_queries: how many calories in cheese fries
are cheese fries suitable for hypertension

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo at high confidence (0.95). Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173944 (french fries with cheese sauce) as the closest approved-source equivalent, replacing the forbidden delivery-platform source cited in the original input. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care for plain deep-fried french fries, not a direct measurement for cheese fries. Overall confidence is medium due to the composite nature of the dish and the need for source substitution on secondary nutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and moisture data for this composite cheese fries preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173944) as of 2026-04-17. 2. Secondary micronutrient values (calcium 60 mg, iron 0.6 mg, cholesterol 16 mg, saturated fat 6 g, trans fat 0.2 g, vitamin C 4 mg) were originally attributed to a delivery platform in the input record — a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy — and have been replaced with values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 173944 (french fries with cheese sauce) as the closest approved-source equivalent. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate for deep-fried french fries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for cheese fries was found in any approved source. 4. Dietary tag 'plant_based' removed from output as cheese fries contain dairy (cheese sauce) and are not plant-based. 5. Halal status is unverified — cheese sauce may contain non-halal emulsifiers or animal rennet; is_halal set to false pending verification. 6. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 4.52, fat_g 14.56, carbohydrate_g 26.46) are non-zero.

Removed any ambiguous or delivery-platform references from nutrient_source fields. Resolved q4 GI source compliance — gi_source now explicitly names University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) as the approved GI evidence base, with direct DOI URL. No forbidden sources appear in any source field.
